coredata.dc:title: Multi-functional carbon dots for visual detection of picric acid and white-light emission
coredata.dc:creator: T., Vidya
M., Anupama
Muhammed, Sajan
Joseph, Julin
Anappara, Aji A.
coredata.dc:description: Herein we report the synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots (CDs) through microwave-assisted pyrolysis of 2-aminoterephthalic acid and polyethylene glycol. The CDs exhibited relatively high photoluminescence quantum yield of about 67 %, and has been found to be selective and ultrasensitive to the nitroaromatic explosive picric acid, with a detection limit as low as 18 nM. The visual detection of the analyte was realized using CDs in the solid-phase, after coating the dots on silica-based thin-layer chromatographic plates. Furthermore, the broad-band luminescence was exploited for UV-to-visible light conversion, to obtain the color parameters of CIE (0.35, 0.33), CRI of 92 and CCT of 5246 K, representing the cool, white-gamut region of 1931 CIE chromaticity diagram.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Picric acid sensing
Visual detection
White-light emission
Fluorescence
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of nitroaromatic explosives in the aqueous phase
Fabrication of CDs/PVA composite as a phosphor for white LED
CDs as fluorophores for chemical sensing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, University Grants Commission

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the as- prepared carbon dots. The inset shows the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of an individual carbon dot indicating the typical lattice constant.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the aqueous dispersion of CDs, measured using de-ionized water as the reference (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs dispersed in water, collected at different excitations.

(a) Relative variation in luminescence quenching in the presence of different analytes are represented as a bar diagram. The concentrations of the analytes were fixed at 50 μM. (b) PL spectra of the CDs in water at different concentrations of picric acid (zero to 50 μM). The inset shows the Stern–Volmer plot corresponding to the PL intensity. (c) Photographs of the test strips under 365 nm emitting UV lamp for the visual detection of the analyte. (d) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of picric acid.

(a) The spectral compositions estimated from the fluorescence data of the water dispersed nanoparticles at different excitation wavelength (b) The emission spectrum of the UV-LED capped with CDs-dispersed PVA, as obtained using a fibre-coupled spectrophotometer (c) The CIE colour parameters corresponding to the emission spectrum of the capped-LED represented in the chromaticity diagram (with black dot).

head.ce:title: Multi-functional carbon dots for visual detection of picric acid and white-light emission

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Photonic Materials and Devices Laboratory, Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology Calicut (NITC), Kerala, 673601, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Photonic Materials and Devices Laboratory, Department of Physics National Institute of Technology Calicut (NITC), Kerala-673601, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein we report the synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots (CDs) through microwave-assisted pyrolysis of 2-aminoterephthalic acid and polyethylene glycol. The CDs exhibited relatively high photoluminescence quantum yield of about 67 %, and has been found to be selective and ultrasensitive to the nitroaromatic explosive picric acid, with a detection limit as low as 18 nM. The visual detection of the analyte was realized using CDs in the solid-phase, after coating the dots on silica-based thin-layer chromatographic plates. Furthermore, the broad-band luminescence was exploited for UV-to-visible light conversion, to obtain the color parameters of CIE (0.35, 0.33), CRI of 92 and CCT of 5246 K, representing the cool, white-gamut region of 1931 CIE chromaticity diagram.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent carbon dots are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanoparticles, identified for the first time in 2004, during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes [1]. By virtue of their remarkable properties such as low toxicity, high water-solubility, photostability and tailorable surface functionalities, CDs have been exploited in a spectrum of applications including chemical sensing, bioimaging, catalysis and energy conversion applications [2–4]. The optical properties of the CDs, such as fluorescence, phosphorescence, down- and up-conversion photoluminescence root from the recombination of photoexcited electrons at the emissive sites generated due to the hybridization between the carbon backbones and the surface-functional groups [5,6]. During the formation of CDs following bottom-up synthesis methods, the functional groups present in the precursors (or the reaction medium) get attached to the dangling bonds of the carbon core [7–10]. This offers the possibility of custom-tailoring the surface chemistry to suit certain applications via the judicious selection of the precursors; or even in the design of single-pot synthesis protocol of CDs which exhibit multifunctional applications [11–14]. Through the engineering of the surface states on the CDs, broad-band luminescence covering the entire visible range of frequencies upon single-wavelength excitation has been demonstrated [6–8]. The broad-band luminescence of the CDs has been utilized in the fabrication of white-light-emitting diodes (w-LEDs) following remote-phosphor technology, i.e., by incorporating the dots in suitable polymer matrices and then coating the composite on to UV-LED chips [15–19]. Furthermore, the emission traits of the CDs (wavelength as well as quantum yield) are highly sensitive to the chemical environment which the surface-moieties are subjected to. This property has been exploited for the design of CDs-based chemosensors to identify trace quantities of analytes in the solvent medium [20–24].

In this work, we report a simple, one-step route for the fabrication of multifunctional CDs from 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) using microwave-assisted synthesis. The choice of the precursors was done with the intension to introduce amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups on the CDs. The as-prepared CDs demonstrated excellent aqueous solubility, extremely broad-band fluorescence, with a relatively high luminescence quantum yield of ∼ 67 % (λex =410 nm). We have investigated the versatility of the CDs in diverse applications such as a chemosensor for the detection of nitroaromatic explosives and as a UV-to-visible light converter phosphor for solid-state lighting. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs in aqueous dispersion was found to selectively decrease in the presence of picric acid (PA), which was utilized for luminescence quenching-based detection of PA. The broad-band luminescence of the CDs was preserved even after dispersing the dots in solid-state polymer matrices, and hence the nanodots-dispersed polymer has been exploited as the phosphor to convert UV electroluminescence to white light with Commission Internationale de L’Eclairage (CIE) parameter of (0.35, 0.33).

Materials used include 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA), polyethylene glycol (PEG) and orthophosphoric acid (H3PO4). ATA was purchased from Alfa Aesar (India), PEG and H3PO4 were obtained from Merck Millipore. Dialysis tubing having molecular weight cut-off 1 kDa was supplied by Spectrum Laboratories (USA). The ultrapure water provided by Labostar TWF water purification system (18.2 MΩ cm, Siemens Ultrapure Water Systems) was used for all experiments.

Microwave-assisted pyrolysis was adopted for the synthesis of carbon dots. In a typical experiment, we used 0.05 g of ATA and mixed with 0.2 g of PEG, followed by addition of 10 mL of H3PO4 under stirring conditions. The solution was then subjected to microwave irradiation at 800 W for 5 min. Upon microwave heating, the colourless solution turned dark brown, which was further dispersed in water and was then centrifuged at 14000 rpm for 45 min to remove the bulk, unreacted materials. The supernatant was further dialyzed (using dialysis tubing having molecular weight cut-off 1 kDa) against ultrapure water for two days, and the nanoparticles left inside the dialysis tube were collected for further characterization. The aqueous dispersion was subjected to lyophilization (using Labconco Freezone 1 L Benchtop Freeze Dry System, USA) to obtain powdered samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of nitroaromatic explosives in the aqueous phase
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solutions of 5 × 10−4 M analytes such as benzoic acid, 4-nitrophenol, 4-methoxy benzoic acid, 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene, 2-nitrophenol, 2,4-dinitrophenol, and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol were prepared by dissolving appropriate amounts in a 100 mL volumetric flask, which were then diluted for further measurements. The concentration of CDs used in sensing study was 5 mg/mL as estimated from the thermo-gravimetric analysis, TGA. The excitation wavelength was fixed at 365 nm for all measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CDs/PVA composite as a phosphor for white LED
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate CDs as a UV-to-visible wavelength converter for remote phosphor technology, 5 g of PVA was dissolved in 15 mL water. Then aqueous dispersion of CDs with concentration of 0.01 mg/mL was added under stirring conditions. The UV-LED chip was dipped in to the resulting viscous solution followed by air-drying under room temperature to obtain a thin-film coating on the surface of the LED.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) using JEOL, JEM-2100 F (Japan) were used to study the morphology of the CDs. The functional groups on the surface of the CDs were studied using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, PerkinElmer (Model: Spectra Two) FTIR spectrometer in ATR mode, with a resolution of about 4 cm−1. UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out using PerkinElmer Lambda 35 spectrophotometer and the steady-state fluorescence spectra were obtained using Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). The luminescence life-time measurements were performed using a Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba JobinYvon Inc, France) using time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique. A fiber-coupled spectrometer (USB4000, Ocean Optics, USA) was used to measure the emission spectra of phosphor-capped UV-LEDs.

The samples for TEM were prepared by drop-casting aqueous dispersion of nanoparticles onto a copper grid. The spectrophotometric and fluorescence studies were done after dispersing the nanoparticles in ultrapure water. FTIR was carried out using powder samples, collected from the aqueous dispersion after lyophilization. CIE parameters were calculated from fluorescence data using Osram Sylvania color calculator software [25].

The surface morphological studies of the carbon dots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). A representative TEM image of the carbon dots is included in Fig. 1 , which shows non-uniformity in the particle size distribution; with smaller dots in the size range 3−10 nm, and also the presence of agglomerated, larger particles. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of a typical carbon dot is depicted in the inset of Fig. 1, which exhibits graphitic layers with an inter-planar spacing of 0.32 nm which corresponds to (002) facet of graphite [26].

The FTIR analyses were used to identify the surface chemistry of CDs. The FTIR spectrum of the sample in ATR mode is demonstrated in Fig. S1. The broad band in the region extending from 3333 cm−1 to 3272 cm−1 confirms the presence of OH functional group and NH functional group, meanwhile, the peak at 2914 cm−1 and 2854 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of CH3 and CH2 [27,28]. CO peak at 1748 cm−1 and CC peak at 1644 cm−1 are also present. The peak at 1434 cm−1 may be due to the in-plane OH bending vibration; CO stretching vibration at 1165 cm−1 and COC at 1023 cm−1 are also evident [27,28].

The optical properties of carbon dots in aqueous dispersion were studied using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and steady-state fluorescence measurements. The UV–vis spectrum is displayed in Fig. 2 a, which shows peaks at 206 nm, 233 nm and 320 nm. The absorption peaks at 206 nm and 233 nm may be due to the π-π* transitions and the peak at 320 nm may be ascribed to the n-π* transition of the functional groups present on the CDs [29–31]. The 3D-contour plot of the excitation-emission matrix collected by exciting the sample from 220 nm to 460 nm with 20 nm incremental steps is displayed in Fig. S2. The contour plot shows two prominent peaks at 260 nm and 410 nm; which indicate the presence of two absorbing chromophores: the absorption around 260 nm may be due to sp2 clusters (CC network of the carbon core of CDs) and the peak at 410 nm may be assigned to the emission from the surface states. The fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs at different excitation wavelengths are reported in Fig. 2b. At an excitation wavelength of 300 nm, the emission was observed to be at 410 nm (full-width at half maximum, FWHM ∼132 nm) and up on further increase in the excitation wavelength, the corresponding emission also shifted to higher wavelengths, indicating excitation-wavelength dependent emission. The luminescence quantum yield, QY of the aqueous dispersion of CDs under 410 nm excitation, was estimated to be 67 % (using quinine sulphate as the standard).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs as fluorophores for chemical sensing
ce:section.ce:para: As the presence of electron-donating functionalities such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine groups were confirmed from FTIR analysis, gave the motivation to study the influence of electron-deficient nitroaromatics on the fluorescence of CDs. We have checked the fluorescence intensity variation of aqueous-dispersed CDs in the presence of different analytes such as 4-nitro phenol (4-NP), 2-nitrophenol (2-NP), 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (PA), benzoic acid (BA), phenol, 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP) and 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT). The concentrations of all analytes were fixed at 50 μM. The PL spectra upon addition of the analytes are displayed in Fig. S3; and the corresponding fluorescence intensity variations are represented as a bar plot in Fig. 3 a. The bar plot shows that maximum luminescence quenching occurs in the aqueous dispersion of CDs in the presence of PA. The fluorescence intensity quenches to ∼ 88 % with respect to the blank (without any analyte), in the presence of PA. To analyze the interference of various analytes on the fluorescence of CDs, fluorescence intensity variation of CDs in the presence of picric acid and other analytes were measured. The fluorescence quenching CDs in the presence of the mixture of analytes is included in Fig. S4; which indicated that the luminescence quenching remains intact, even in the presence of other analytes. These results imply that the as-synthesized CDs could be used as a selective and specific sensor for picric acid detection. In order to analyze the sensitivity of CDs, the concentrations of PA were varied and corresponding fluorescence intensity variation was recorded and is depicted in Fig. 3b. The Stern-Volmer plot corresponding to the fluorescence data is shown in the inset of Fig. 3b; which exhibit a linear behaviour in the range 0–50 μM. The quenching constant estimated from the fluorescence-quenching data is about 1.29 × 105 M−1 (R2 = 0.987). The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated around 18 nM; calculated using the equation LOD = 3 σ /S, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample and S is the slope of the calibration curve.

The possibility of the solid-phase detection of PA on contact-mode was also investigated. The test strips were prepared by drop casting 0.5 μL of the aqueous solution of CDs onto thin-layer silica chromatographic plate (TLC plates). As the silica coating on the TLC plate is highly porous, the analyte molecules can be easily diffused into silica plate. Prior to the use of the test strips for the identification of the analyte, we have studied the variation of the luminescence intensity of the CDs upon removal of the water content. Towards this direction, we have dried the drop-casted TLC plate using hot-air blow from a hair dryer (Make: Panasonic, Model: EH-ND21), for 20 min. While drying, the fluorescence was monitored under 365 nm UV-lamp, at different intervals of time, and the digital photographs were collected. The images are included in the Fig. S5. As it is evident from the figure, the luminescence intensity remained the same, without noticeable change throughout the drying process. The fluorescence of the strip was stable even for several days, once the strip was completely dried. The dry test strips were further used for the detection of PA. Picric acid solutions having concentrations of 10 μM, 50 μM were dropped on the spots of the CDs, and was observed under UV light (λ ∼365 nm). The digital photographs corresponding to the fluorescence quenching of the CDs, upon addition of the analyte are demonstrated in Fig. 3c. The fluorescence of CDs in the test strips diminished considerably, with the addition of PA, which can be observed with naked eyes, under UV illumination. This visual change of fluorescence intensity indicates that CDs could detect PA even in the solid-phase, on contact-mode.

The next step is to identify the mechanism behind the remarkable fluorescence quenching of CDs in aqueous dispersion, upon addition of the analyte. The quenching of a fluorophore upon addition of the analyte generally occurs through processes such as: (i) electronic energy transfer, EET (ii) inner filter effect, IFE (iii) formation of donor-acceptor complex between the fluorophore and the analyte, or (iv) through intermolecular interactions [26,32,33]. Typically, the first two quenching mechanisms: electronic energy transfer and inner filter effect, require a significant overlap between the emission spectra of CDs and the absorption spectra of the analytes [32,33]. The UV–vis absorption of the aqueous solution of picric acid and the emission spectrum of CDs are displayed in Fig. S6; which exhibits a mild overlap. The inner filter effect occurs when the emitted energy of fluorophores is absorbed by the analytes, even in the absence of any direct interaction between excited fluorophore and quencher molecule. Thus, in the quenching process due to this mechanism, the emission wavelength as well as the luminescence life-time remains unaltered, before and after addition of analytes [32,33]. In contrast, in the electronic energy transfer phenomena, the donor fluorophores in the excited state, transfer its energy to the nearby acceptor chromophores (analyte). In this process not only the fluorescence intensity, but also the emission life-time of the donor may be altered. For the confirmation of EET, fluorescence life-time measurements of CDs were performed using time-correlated single-photon counting technique (TCSPC). The concentrations of picric acid were chosen to be 0 M (blank), 0.5 μM and 2 μM. The excitation wavelength used was 370 nm and the emission was collected at 460 nm. The corresponding decay curve is shown in Fig. 3d. The average emission life-time was obtained from the fit of the TCSPC data to be about 12.36 ns. The fit parameters are included in the Table-S1 (in Supplementary Information). From the data, it was noted that the average life-time remained the same even in the absence or the presence of the analyte, which hints that the possibility of EET can be excluded. From Fig. S3, it is evident that in the presence of picric acid the emission wavelength changes from 465 nm to 478 nm. Owing to the shift in emission wavelength, we exclude the possibility of IFE as the luminescence quenching mechanism; since in the inner filter effect, the emission wavelength and life-time are identical before and after the addition of analytes.

Another possible quenching mechanism could be the complex formation between CDs and PA, which can be expected as the CDs contain electron-rich surface-functional groups. Upon complex formation between the fluorophore and the analyte, the absorption spectrum of the former get perturbed and spectral shift or appearance of new peaks can be expected. To study the possibility of complex formation, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the aqueous solution of CDs was collected with and without the presence of different concentrations of PA. The absorption spectra were obtained with different concentrations of picric acid is included in Fig. S7. With the addition of the analyte, an absorption peak appears around 350 nm; which might be the absorption signature of the analyte (picric acid) itself; also, there is a slight shift in the peaks at 206 nm to 208 nm; and that at 320 nm to 323 nm. These results imply that the analyte may form a non-fluorescent complex with the electron-rich surface functional groups on the CDs, leading to fluorescence quenching [32,33]. Furthermore, there is an additional possibility of some molecular interactions such as hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl group of picric acid and NH2, NH and OH sites present on the CDs, which might lead to emission quenching [32,33]. The redshift in the emission spectra after addition of picric acid and the order of quenching performance of PA, with respect to DNP and NP (as the quenching efficacy of PA > DNP > NP), is an indication of hydrogen bond interactions between CDs and picric acid [26,34,35]. But these interactions typically show a very mild contribution to luminescence quenching. In contrast to other analytes used in the study, PA act as a strong protonic acid in aqueous solution, due to the bonding of three electron-withdrawing nitro groups on the benzene ring. The interaction between the electron-rich functional groups on the carbon dot and picric acid moiety was previously suggested by Q. Niu et al., [36] where the anion – cation pair, of PA− and carbon dot-NH2+ is formed via strong electrostatic interaction between the two. The selectivity of PA among similar members, vis., 4 NP and DNP can be explained by the lowest pKa value among these analytes, which obviously indicates stronger interaction between PA and amine functionalized carbon dot, well explaining the excellent selectivity of carbon dot towards the former [37].

The aqueous dispersion of CDs exhibits broad-band emission spectra (FWHM ∼ 130 nm), which covers the entire visible range, for excitation wavelengths in the range, 300−380 nm (Fig. 2b). Using the luminescence data, we calculated spectral composition of the emission, collected at the excitation wavelengths (λex = 340, 360, and 380 nm), by dividing the emission spectra into six colors (violet, blue, green, yellow, orange and red) [15–18]. The spectral compositions estimated using the luminescence data are demonstrated in the Fig. 4 a. The ratio of blue to red intensity to the total intensity is considered as the spectral component ratio; and for an excitation wavelength of 340 nm its magnitude is 64 %; meanwhile at 360 nm excitation it increases to 69 % and for 380 nm excitation it increases further to 74 %. The significant high magnitude of spectral component ratio indicates that the CDs may be utilized for remote-phosphor applications.

To study the possibility of CDs as a phosphor in solid-state lighting, the CDs were dispersed in a host medium, poly vinyl alcohol (PVA) which is transparent to both the excitation and visible emission ranges. The polymer-dispersed CDs were capped on to a 365 nm UV-LED chip. The resultant emission of the capped LED is illustrated in Fig. 4b. The CIE coordinates of the resultant emission were estimated using Osram Sylvania software, to be about (0.35, 0.33). The CIE coordinates corresponding to the emission of the CDs-capped LED is extremely close to that of spectrally pure white-light, for which the parameters are (0.33, 0.33). The color rendering index (CRI) and correlated color temperature (CCT) of the emission spectra were calculated to be about 92 and 5246 K respectively. The chromaticity diagram with the CIE coordinates for the white LED is depicted in Fig. 4c. These results imply that CDs could be used as an excellent UV-to-visible color converter (phosphor) material, to obtain cool, white-light emission.

In conclusion, we report a simple, microwave-assisted technique to fabricate CDs using 2-aminoterephtalic acid and polyethylene glycol as the precursors. The as-synthesized carbon dots show excellent aqueous solubility and exhibit broad-band emission, which covers a significant fraction of the visible range, under UV excitation; with a fluorescence quantum yield of 67 % (λex =410 nm). The fluorescence intensity of the dots was found to specifically and selectively quench in the presence of picric acid, a nitroaromatic explosive and environmental pollutant. This feature was utilized to develop chemosensors based on the aqueous dispersion of CDs, as well as in the visual-detection using the solid-state on contact-mode sensing of PA. The estimated lowest detection limit in the aqueous dispersion of CDs was estimated to be about 18 nM. The broad-band luminescence of the CDs was preserved even after dispersing the CDs in a polymer host matrix (PVA); was thus employed in realizing the white-light generation. The CDs containing PVA was capped on to a UV-LED chip, which emits 365 nm. The color parameters of the resultant emission from the capped LED obtained are CIE (0.35, 0.33), CRI = 92 and CCT =5246 K, representing cool-white emission.
The corresponding author is responsible for ensuring that the descriptions are accurate and agreed by all authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial assistance from UGC-DAE CSR Kalpakkam Node, under the CRS project scheme (CSR-KN/CRS-106/2018-19/1045).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2021.111223.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

New J. Chem.

J. Appl. Phys.
